context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Markets and Marketization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How are market institutions different from the state? What do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them? How can you work jointly within different types of organizations and what are the challenges you might face by doing that? How does marketization — the process of creating markets — happen and what mistakes should it avoid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i'm stuck on this reflection abt markets vs the state in development planning, and idk how to start tbh. can u help me figure out what the prompt is really asking?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's dig in. Before we talk about the prompt's structure, tell me this: in your own words, what do you think is the most fundamental difference between a market and a state? Just take a shot at it — there's no wrong answer here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs orientation without me giving away the structure or content of the answer. I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core distinction the prompt is asking about, rather than explaining it to them. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them lead with their own thinking first.

exchanges.student: uh maybe markets are like businesses/money stuff and the state is the govt? ngl that feels way too basic.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a reasonable starting point — you're identifying two different kinds of institutions. Now push it a bit further: what drives each one? Like, what motivates a market actor versus what motivates the state to do what it does?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a surface-level answer and is self-aware about it. I should validate that they're on the right track without confirming or expanding the answer for them. A Socratic follow-up question pushing them to think about what specifically makes these two things operate differently — their logic, their rules, their incentives — will move them toward a more substantive understanding.

exchanges.student: idk, profit for markets and maybe rules or public good for the state? also quick check, that first vague try didnt get me unstuck lol.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually getting somewhere useful — profit vs. public interest is a real and important distinction. Now think about this: if markets are driven by profit, what does that mean for how they behave? Does profit motive make them reliable partners for development goals, or does it create complications?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good intuition about incentives (profit vs. public good/rules) and is also expressing mild frustration. I should acknowledge their progress genuinely without flattering, and build on their answer with a sharper Socratic question that connects incentives to the assignment's deeper question about what you need to KNOW about markets if you want to work with them. That's the next logical step in the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah probs both, but can u just explain the whole thing kinda broadly first bc i’m still fuzzy rn?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — if I lay it all out, you won't be doing the thinking the assignment requires. But honestly? You're not as fuzzy as you think. You've already identified that markets are profit-driven and states aim at public interest. That's a real insight. So stick with it: you said markets are 'probably both' reliable and complicated as partners — what's one specific complication you can think of?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the content for them, which would violate the core rule of never giving the answer directly and not completing tasks for the student. I need to firmly but kindly redirect them back to the Socratic process. They are actually making progress — their last answer was good — so I should point that out and keep pushing them forward with a focused question.

exchanges.student: maybe they care abt ppl only if it makes money, but i'm still not seeing how that connects to marketization fr.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — markets may ignore people when serving them isn't profitable. Now use that: if that's how markets naturally behave, what do you think could go wrong when a government tries to CREATE a market where one didn't exist before?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good observation about markets only caring about people when it's profitable — this is essentially the externalities/public goods problem. I should affirm this briefly and then use it as a bridge to marketization, which is the next part of the prompt. A targeted Socratic question connecting their insight to the process of creating markets will move the conversation forward efficiently.

exchanges.student: maybe it leaves out poor ppl or turns basic needs into stuff u can only get if u can pay? tbh i'm also lost on the "work jointly within different types of organizations" part.
exchanges.tutor: Good — excluding the poor or commodifying basic needs are real risks worth developing. On 'working jointly': think about what you've already said — markets want profit, states want public good. If a planner has to work with BOTH at the same time, what kind of tension might they run into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress on marketization pitfalls and is now asking about a different part of the prompt. I should briefly affirm their marketization insight and then pivot to their new question with a Socratic probe rather than explaining it. The 'work jointly' part is about navigating different institutional logics — market vs. state vs. NGOs etc. — which connects back to what they've already been building.

exchanges.student: maybe diff goals, diff timelines, diff accountability? ngl can u give me a simple outline bc i'm bouncing between parts.
exchanges.tutor: Strong observations — goals, timelines, accountability are all worth exploring in your paper. But I won't build the outline for you. Here's what I'd suggest instead: look at the four questions in the prompt and match what we've discussed to each one. What would YOU put under each question as a starting point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent progress — different goals, timelines, and accountability is a genuinely strong set of distinctions. However, they're asking for an outline, which would mean me doing the organizational work for them. I should decline clearly but redirect them: since we've now touched on most parts of the prompt, this is a good moment to ask them to try synthesizing what they've gathered into their own rough structure.

exchanges.student: ok maybe for q1 i’d say markets allocate thru prices/profit and the state rules/governs for public goals, but idk if that’s too simplistic. q3 is still messing me up tho.
exchanges.tutor: Q1 is a solid foundation — don't overthink it. On Q3, you already said different organizations have different goals, timelines, and accountability. So here's the question: if YOU were a planner working with both a private firm and a government agency on the same project, whose priorities would you prioritize when they conflict, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid answer for Q1 and is self-critical about it, which is good intellectual honesty. I should briefly affirm it without over-praising, and then focus on Q3 since that's where they're stuck. Q3 is about working jointly across different organizations — I should ask a targeted question that builds on what they already said about tensions (goals, timelines, accountability) to help them think through what a planner actually does in that situation.

exchanges.student: i guess the public one? but then why work w/ market actors at all lol. that angle still feels messy.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling IS the point — sit with it for a second. What might markets offer that the state can't do as well on its own? That might answer your own 'why bother' question. And after that, I'd encourage you to start putting your ideas into a rough draft — you have more than enough to begin writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually stumbling onto a really productive tension — why involve markets at all if public good is the priority? This is essentially the efficiency vs. equity tradeoff at the heart of development planning. I should push them to answer their own question rather than resolving it for them. This will also help them see that the 'messiness' is actually the substance of Q3. We're getting close to the end of the planned conversation length, so I should also nudge them toward integrating everything into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: maybe efficiency, investment, innovation? but yeah i'm still tempted to want the big picture all at once tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — efficiency, investment, and innovation are real reasons to bring markets in, even when public goals lead. You now have ideas covering every part of the prompt. The big picture you're looking for will actually become clearer once you start writing — that's how reflection pieces work. Try a rough draft and come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all major parts of the prompt through our discussion: markets vs. state, what to know about markets, working jointly, and marketization pitfalls. Their last answer about efficiency, investment, and innovation is genuinely good. The temptation for the 'big picture all at once' is understandable but I should not give it to them. Instead, since we've hit the planned conversation length and they have enough material, I should firmly push them toward writing a draft and remind them that the big picture will emerge through their own writing process.
